findings: The GB200 NVL72 rack generates over 120 kW of heat — 5× to 15× beyond the 8–25 kW air-cooling limit for a standard server rack — making air cooling physically impossible.
The B200 GPU die operates at 500–600 W/cm² heat flux, which is 40–100× beyond the 5–15 W/cm² effective heat flux that air-cooled heatsinks can handle.
Air-cooled heatsinks achieve a thermal resistance of 0.1–0.3 °C/W; at 1200 W per GB200 module, this would produce 120–360 °C above ambient — far exceeding the GB200's maximum junction temperature.

Even if air cooling were thermally feasible for the GB200 NVL72, the fan power required to move sufficient airflow through a 120 kW rack would consume 20–30% of the rack's compute power and generate 90+ dB of noise.
NVIDIA has officially confirmed that GB200 NVL72 requires liquid cooling; this is described as a mandatory architectural requirement, not a design preference.
